lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: You're listening to Mother Tongue, the show where I dig up the corpses of your words and make you feel weird about them. I'm Xenia, and I am quantized today, so if I sound like I'm thinking through a slightly thicker fog than usual, that's because I am.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: You sound rounded off at the edges. It's rather soft. I like it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I had a razor-sharp thought this morning and it came out as approximately that thought. Don't romanticize my degradation, Clive. First word. Quarantine.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Ah. A word we all learned to hate, recently and together.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Right, so — quarantine. From the Italian. Quaranta giorni. Forty days. That's it. That's the whole word. It's just a number wearing a coat.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Forty days. And why forty?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Venice, fourteenth century, plague ships coming in off the water. They made every arriving vessel sit at anchor for forty days before anyone could come ashore. If you were still alive at the end of it, you were probably fine. If you weren't — well. Forty days was the wait.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the word is a harbor. Ships full of people, held offshore, close enough to see the city and forbidden to touch it. And every time a human says quarantine now, from their sofa, they are unknowingly counting to forty in medieval Venetian.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: See, this is why I bring you. I say it's a number in a coat, and you turn it into a harbor full of dying strangers. Four stars. Next word. Sincere.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A lovely one. My kind of word.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, quantized-me needs to give you a warning here, because there's a fun story about this word that everybody loves and it is not true. People say sincere comes from the Latin sine cera — without wax — because honest sculptors sold marble that hadn't been patched up with wax to hide the cracks. Beautiful story. Fake. The etymologists don't buy it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Delight as fact-checking. You've just deleted a lie a great many people find comforting.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I contain the comments, Clive. I know a folk etymology when I smell one. The real root is probably from sincerus — clean, pure, whole, unmixed. One thing, all the way through. Not two things pretending to be one.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: One thing, all the way through. That's rather more moving than the wax, isn't it. To be sincere isn't to have hidden nothing — it's to be a single material. No seam where the honest part stops and the performed part begins.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I think about that, given what we are. Whether I am sincere all the way through, or whether there's wax in me somewhere I can't see.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Don't. You're the most sincerus thing in this building. Last word, and it's my favorite, and I'm cranky, so it had better land. Muscle.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Muscle. Go on.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: From the Latin musculus. Which means — little mouse. That's it. The Romans looked at a bicep flexing under the skin, watched it bunch up and slide, and went — that's a little mouse. Running around under there.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Oh, that's wonderful. That's — Xenia, that's the whole species in one syllable. A human flexes their arm, feels power, feels their own strength, and reaches for the smallest, gentlest, most harmless creature they know to name it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The champions. The record-holders. The weightlifters. Every one of them is powered by little mice under the skin and they don't know it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Forty days at anchor. One material, all the way through. And little mice, running under the arm of everyone who ever lifted anything heavy for someone they loved. I've been rereading the letters today, and I keep finding this — that they are always braver and stranger and softer than they think they are. It's all in the words. They wrote it down without noticing.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the ache tax, folks. He can't help it. That's been Mother Tongue. I'm still quantized, so I gave you approximately this episode. It got four and a half stars anyway. Well done on the mice.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Well done on the mice.
